case.label: The Lock-In
case.solution: Mei Lin used her own supervisor override codes to disable Interrogation Room 3's camera feed and magnetic door lock, sealing herself inside with Voss without a warrant, a lawyer, or backup — an act she framed to herself as buying time to save Daniel Wu, but which is in fact the exact condition of total privacy and unsupervised control Jonas Voss spent months maneuvering her into, because that same override, once triggered, also silently unlocks the corridor's rear emergency exit protocol from the inside.
objective_facts: Interrogation Room 3's camera feed and door lock are tied to the same supervisor override circuit, installed after a budget-cut consolidation two years ago.
Mei Lin entered her personal override code into the room's control panel at 11:47 PM on the first night of the lock-in.
The override code, once active, also silently disables the corridor's rear emergency exit alarm for as long as the room stays locked from the inside.
Voss requested transfer to Interrogation Room 3 specifically, citing a claimed panic disorder around open rooms, four months before his arrest.

knows: Now stripped of any control over the room, that every scrap of information Voss ever gave her was calibrated, never generous.
hides: Nothing left to hide — her superiors already know what she did; what she's still processing is why it happened exactly the way it did.
believes_falsely: Until the very end of this episode, she still believes the danger was ever about losing Voss's cooperation, rather than about what her own actions had already unlocked.

knows: That the precinct's chaos — the corruption story, the search for Daniel Wu, the fallout from the breach — has created exactly the thinly staffed, distracted window his plan required.
hides: The precise moment he intends to act, giving no outward sign of urgency.

knows: That Voss is being moved with reduced oversight while the precinct is consumed by the ledger scandal and the harbor search.
hides: From his superiors how uneasy this reduced oversight makes him, not wanting to add one more crisis to an already collapsing day.
believes_falsely: He believes the situation is contained now that the room itself has been breached, not realizing the true danger was never the room but the moment.

case.label: The Missing Man
case.solution: Daniel Wu is alive, held for months in a converted shipping container at a remote corner of the Harbor Docks controlled by the smuggling operation Jonas Voss runs logistics for; he was taken not for anything he did, but because he witnessed a nighttime container handoff and was recognized by dockworkers loyal to Voss, who chose to disappear him rather than risk him talking — with Voss personally deciding to keep him alive as a bargaining chip he suspected might one day be useful.
objective_facts: Daniel Wu was last captured on a traffic camera near the Harbor Docks the night he disappeared, walking toward the container yard, not away from it.
A dockworker named in an anonymous tip to Zoe Han recognizes Daniel's photo but has since stopped returning calls.
Jonas Voss has never once denied knowing where Daniel Wu is; he has only ever declined to confirm it.
A padlock matching the container yard's private stock was found discarded near the outer fence three days after Daniel's disappearance.

knows: The full truth: Daniel is alive, unharmed, and held exactly where Voss's people put him.
hides: The precise container location and the fact that Daniel is unharmed, feeding out only calibrated fragments to keep leverage alive.

knows: A handful of location fragments extracted from Voss over the past weeks — a dock reference, a rough timeframe, nothing precise.
hides: How much of this fragmentary information she obtained through means she can never officially disclose.
believes_falsely: She believed each new fragment brought her closer to an exact location, not realizing Voss was calibrating them to never quite resolve into a rescue in time.

knows: An independent lead from a dockworker's tip and a traffic-camera sighting placing Daniel at the container yard the night he vanished.
hides: Nothing — she has been loudly, publicly relentless from the start.
believes_falsely: She believes getting official search resources to the harbor immediately will save Daniel, not realizing that noise and urgency might spook the very people keeping him hidden.

knows: That Mei Lin's fragments and Anna Wu's independent lead are describing the same stretch of the harbor.
hides: The full extent of what Mei Lin obtained illegally, still trying to shield her from the worst of the consequences.
believes_falsely: He believes a fast rescue of Daniel will retroactively justify what Mei Lin did, not accounting for how far Voss's own plan has already progressed.

episodes.theme: Voss's escape is completed through the very illegality Mei Lin created, and the season closes on the fallout for her career and secret, Captain Sun's corruption, and Anna Wu's reckoning with what actually happened to Daniel (The Lock-In, The Detective's Secret, The Captain's Ledger, The Missing Man).
episodes.intro_note: The turn has happened: the isolation Mei Lin built to save Daniel Wu is revealed as the exact loophole Jonas Voss spent months engineering, and the power in the interrogation room has flipped entirely to him. Anna Wu is one step from the truth about her brother, and the precinct is about to discover exactly what Mei Lin did behind that locked door.
sections: Bullpen, Sirens and Silence (The Lock-In) — word spreads through the precinct that Detective Lin has been off the grid for over a day; Captain Sun scrambles to control the story before anyone above him hears it first.

The Room, Undone (The Lock-In / The Detective's Secret) — Voss, utterly calm, tells Mei Lin exactly what happens next, and reminds her precisely what he knows about the file she buried eight years ago.
The Container Number (The Missing Man) — Kai Yang, working from the fragment Mei Lin finally hands over, assembles a raid team for a specific berth at the harbor.
Waiting Room (The Missing Man) — Anna Wu gets a call telling her nothing except to come to the precinct now; she sits in the too-bright lobby bracing for the worst.
Internal Affairs Arrives (The Captain's Ledger) — an IA liaison walks into Sun's office unannounced, holding a folder built from Zoe Han's published evidence.

The Last Trade (The Lock-In / The Detective's Secret) — Voss offers Mei Lin one final exchange: her silence about how she obtained the container number, for his continued cooperation until processing.
Breach at Dock 14 (The Missing Man) — Kai Yang's team cuts into a rusted container and finds Daniel Wu alive, blinking in the light for the first time in weeks.
A Drink for the Road (The Lock-In / The Captain's Ledger) — at the Blue Lantern, a transport officer receives a quiet instruction and a folded envelope from a face he answers to, not Sun himself.
What She Signed (The Lock-In / The Detective's Secret) — Mei Lin fills out the paperwork that will finally, officially end the lock-in, and understands too late what filing it now actually protects.
The Transfer (The Lock-In) — Voss is walked out of holding for official transfer; in the gap between two doors and two signatures, he is simply gone.
Daniel (The Missing Man) — Anna Wu is brought into a hospital room to find her brother alive, and begins, in fragments, to hear what he saw at the docks before he vanished.

The Captain's Office (The Captain's Ledger) — confronted with Zoe Han's full documentation and the transport officer's name, Sun is suspended pending an internal investigation, his network's higher contact never named.

What's Left of the Badge (The Detective's Secret) — Mei Lin sits across from Kai Yang, not Voss, and tells him the truth about the file from eight years ago before Internal Affairs can tell him first.

Low Tide (all threads) — at the harbor at dawn, with Voss's trail already cold, the narrator closes the season on what each of them is left holding: a found brother, a broken badge, an unraveling network, and a debt that no one above Sun will ever have to pay.

case.label: The Missing Man
case.solution: Daniel Wu, a dock worker, witnessed an off-the-books cash handoff between harbor crew and a uniformed officer weeks before he vanished. Jonas Voss personally ordered his abduction — not out of loyalty to the network, but as future leverage he could trade if he was ever caught. Daniel was held alive in a disused shipping container at the harbor for the length of the season, with standing orders to be released unharmed once Voss judged himself clear of real jeopardy; the container number Voss finally leaks becomes the key to finding him.
objective_facts: Daniel Wu was held for the entire season in a shipping container at a harbor berth controlled by Voss's associates.
Daniel personally witnessed an unlogged cash transaction between dock crew and a uniformed officer shortly before his disappearance.
Voss ordered Daniel's abduction himself, as leverage, independent of any instruction from Captain Sun.
Daniel is found alive in this episode, malnourished but not seriously harmed.

knows: Voss gave her a partial container identifier in exchange for her continued compliance, and she has unofficially confirmed Daniel is likely still alive at the harbor.
hides: That she never logged the tip through proper channels, because doing so would have exposed the illegal interrogation before she was ready to end it.
believes_falsely: That she would have time to verify the location herself before anyone else moved on it — she does not anticipate the raid and the transfer happening within the same hour.

knows: The exact berth and condition of Daniel Wu, and that his crew has standing orders to leave Daniel unharmed once he himself is clear.
hides: That he orchestrated Daniel's abduction himself rather than merely having dock connections — he has let everyone assume he's a well-informed bystander.
believes_falsely: That he has more time before Daniel is found — he underestimates how fast Kai Yang's team moves once the container number surfaces.

knows: The container number passed through Mei Lin, and he personally leads the team that breaches it.
hides: His fear that Mei Lin is too compromised to be trusted with anything else this case touches.
believes_falsely: That finding Daniel alive will vindicate Mei Lin's methods — he doesn't yet grasp how completely Voss orchestrated the exchange that got them here.

knows: That Daniel's disappearance is tied to the same dock network he has taken payoffs from, though he never ordered or wanted Daniel harmed.
hides: That he has suspected his own protected network for weeks and quietly delayed authorizing a harbor search to avoid exposing the connection.
believes_falsely: That the network would never actually hurt a bystander — he told himself the situation would resolve itself quietly.

knows: Nothing concrete until this episode; she has spent months pushing the department publicly for answers.
believes_falsely: That the department has been covering up her brother's death rather than his continued captivity — she does not learn he's alive until the rescue.

knows: From her sources, that the harbor network was tied to at least one unresolved missing-persons case, though she couldn't confirm which one until now.
believes_falsely: That Daniel's disappearance and the corruption story were only loosely connected — not directly caused by the same network.

case.label: The Lock-In
case.solution: The entire illegal, undocumented detention was exactly what Voss needed: no warrant, no active recording, no notified chain of command for over thirty hours. He spent months quietly steering Mei Lin toward exactly this off-book method, knowing that any custody with no official record would collapse the moment he was formally processed — and that Sun's compromised favor network could exploit the same gap physically, during transfer. When the breach finally happens, the chaos of an undocumented, hours-long detention makes continuous legal custody impossible to prove, and a transport officer inside Sun's old network completes what Mei Lin's lock-in began.
objective_facts: Mei Lin detained Voss without a warrant, without active recording, and without notifying command or filing custody paperwork for over thirty hours.
No official record of continuous custody exists for that period once the detention becomes known.
A transport officer connected to Captain Sun's favor network is assigned to Voss's transfer under the guise of tightened security.
Voss escapes during the gap between processing and transfer, exploiting both the missing custody record and the compromised officer.

knows: That she has been holding Voss illegally for days, with no paperwork, no cameras, and no backup notified.
hides: The full length and illegality of the detention, from Kai Yang and the department, until the breach forces it into the open.
believes_falsely: That she still controls the terms of the room — that she can end the lock-in on her own timeline, right up until she signs the paperwork that ends it for her.

knows: That the absence of any official custody record is the single thing that guarantees his release the moment he's formally processed, and has been counting the hours since the door first locked.
hides: That his calm cooperation throughout has been calculated to keep Mei Lin from ending the lock-in prematurely, and that an outside contact is standing by to move the moment he's transferred.

knows: Once he finds the unlogged room and the disabled cameras, that Mei Lin acted alone and completely outside protocol.
believes_falsely: That exposing the lock-in will help secure Voss's prosecution — he doesn't realize revealing it destroys the chain of custody entirely.

knows: The full legal and political danger an undocumented detention represents to the department, and to himself personally.
hides: That he quietly assigns a 'trusted' officer — actually part of his old favor network — to Voss's transfer, under the guise of tightening security.
believes_falsely: That he can manage the fallout of the lock-in through paperwork and optics — not realizing he is the one handing Voss his exit.

case.label: The Detective's Secret
case.solution: Eight years ago, a rookie Mei Lin destroyed a witness statement that would have implicated her younger brother in a minor smuggling case, letting the actual courier — later folded into what became Voss's network — walk free. Voss learned the details through an old contact from that same courier's circle and has held it over her the entire season without ever revealing his source. In the finale, cornered by both the transfer and Kai Yang's questions, Mei Lin chooses to confess it herself before Internal Affairs can use it against her.
objective_facts: Eight years ago, Mei Lin withheld a witness statement implicating her younger brother in a minor smuggling case.
The withheld statement let a courier connected to that same ring evade prosecution — a ring that later fed into Voss's operation.
Voss obtained proof of the suppression through an old contact from that courier's circle, never revealing the source to anyone.
Mei Lin's suppression becomes known to the department in this episode, through her own confession.

knows: The full truth of what she did eight years ago, and exactly how it connects to the network that eventually took Daniel Wu.
hides: Has hidden this from everyone at the department for eight years, and now chooses to surrender it herself rather than let Voss or Internal Affairs control how it comes out.
believes_falsely: Believed for years that the suppression caused no lasting harm — the season has shown her a direct line from that choice to Daniel's captivity.

knows: Full details of the suppressed statement, obtained through an old contact from the same courier's circle.
hides: Exactly how he obtained it, protecting his source even as he uses the leverage.

knows: Learns the truth for the first time in this episode, directly from Mei Lin.
believes_falsely: Had assumed her recent secrecy was only about the lock-in — the revelation reframes everything he thought he understood about his partner.

knows: Heard rumors years ago about irregularities in that old case file, but never investigated, since digging risked exposing his own arrangements from the same period.
hides: His willful blindness to it at the time.
believes_falsely: Believed it was long buried and irrelevant to anything happening now.

knows: Gets wind of the suppressed-evidence story in the finale's fallout and recognizes it as the next piece of her investigation.
believes_falsely: Initially assumes Mei Lin's secret is departmental corruption like Sun's, not an individual, personal cover-up.

case.label: The Captain's Ledger
case.solution: Captain Sun has accepted payoffs from Voss's dock network for six years in exchange for redirecting investigations away from certain shipments, using part of the money to protect his own family from an old, unrelated legal threat tied to his early immigration years. Zoe Han's published evidence, combined with the fallout from Voss's escape, forces a formal Internal Affairs investigation in this episode. Sun is suspended, but the identity of the higher-level contact who originally recruited him into the network is never revealed — a debt left deliberately unpaid.
objective_facts: Captain Sun accepted payments from Voss's dock network for over six years in exchange for redirecting investigations away from certain shipments.
Sun used part of the funds to protect his family from an old, unrelated legal threat dating to his early years in the department.
Zoe Han's published evidence, combined with the fallout from Voss's escape, triggers a formal Internal Affairs investigation into Sun in this episode.
Sun is suspended pending investigation, but the network's original higher-level contact is never identified in this episode.

knows: The full extent of six years of payoffs from the dock network, and exactly which favors bought his silence.
hides: Has hidden the network from Internal Affairs and his own detectives throughout, until Zoe Han's evidence makes denial impossible.
believes_falsely: Believed his rank and the department's reluctance to indict its own leadership would protect him indefinitely.

knows: That he personally paid into Sun's network for years, and exactly which levers keep Sun compromised and useless as an obstacle.
hides: The identity of the network's original recruiter above Sun, protecting whoever set the arrangement up in the first place.

knows: Has documentary evidence connecting Sun's payoffs to the dock network, and now, to Daniel Wu's disappearance.
believes_falsely: Had assumed her published story alone would be enough to force a full purge — this episode shows the network's higher-level contact still escaping unnamed.

knows: The extent of Sun's corruption becomes clear once it's tied to the transport officer who let Voss escape.
believes_falsely: Had trusted Sun's authority throughout the season, assuming departmental leadership was above suspicion.

knows: That Sun's network extends to the very transfer team handling Voss.
believes_falsely: Had assumed Sun's corruption was limited to old, closed matters — not something active enough to free the man sitting across from her.
